docs.title: How to Enroll Your Mobile Device in MDM (Microsoft Endpoint Manager)
Overview
docs.text:
If you do not enroll your mobile device with Microsoft Endpoint Manager (MEM), formerly named Intune, you will not be able to access company email or data on your mobile device.

Important Note: Enrolling your phone will not give the company the ability to wipe your personal data, control your phone, or retrieve your phone number. It creates a "sandbox" on your phone where apps and accounts held in that sandbox can securely interact with company email and cloud data.

The MEM Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution will: - Prevent you from copying company data outside of the sandbox of managed apps on your device. - Require you to install and use approved apps to read email (Outlook mobile app) and interact with data (OneDrive and SharePoint).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before beginning enrollment, note the following:

  • If you already have your company email account on your phone, you'll need to remove it first.
  • iPhone directions for removing account
  • Android directions for removing account
  • Install the Company Portal App from your appropriate App Store before beginning enrollment. Read through the enrollment directions below before logging into the Company Portal.
  • Read the full directions and watch the video at the enrollment link below before executing the steps.

docs.title: After Enrollment: Installing Required Apps
docs.text: After completing enrollment and installing the Outlook app from the Company Portal, you will also need to install the Contacts App so you can sync your contacts back to your phone.

docs.title: Enabling Contact Syncing in Outlook (iPhone)
docs.text:
1. Open the Outlook app on your iPhone.
  2. Tap your profile icon in the top-left corner, then tap Settings (the gear icon).
  3. Select your Outlook account.
  4. Next to "Save Contacts", toggle the slider to the ON position.
  5. You'll receive a message asking if you'd like to save your Outlook contacts to your iPhone. Ensure your iPhone is plugged into power, then tap "Save to my iPhone".

Note: If you open a link to a Zoom or Teams meeting, you'll need to install that software from the Company Portal app after you enroll.

docs.title: How to Import a Digital Certificate into Outlook
docs.text: This guide explains how to import a digital certificate (.p12 or .pfx file) into Microsoft Outlook for use with email security.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• You must have a .p12 or .pfx digital certificate file (e.g., downloaded from DigiCert) and the password you set for it.
  • Microsoft Outlook must be installed.

docs.title: Step 1: Open Outlook Trust Center
docs.text:
1. In Outlook, click File > Options.
  2. In the Outlook Options dialog box, click Trust Center in the left bar.
  3. Click the Trust Center Settings button.

docs.title: Step 2: Navigate to E-mail Security
docs.text: In the Trust Center dialog box, click E-mail Security in the left bar.

docs.title: Step 3: Open Import/Export
docs.text: Go to the Digital IDs (Certificates) section and click the Import / Export button.

docs.title: Step 4: Import the Certificate
docs.text:
In the Import / Export Digital ID dialog box:

  1. Select Import existing Digital ID from a file.
  2. Click the Browse button. In the Locate Security Profile dialog box, select your .p12 file (the one downloaded from DigiCert), then click Open.
  3. Enter the certificate password in the Password box.
  4. Enter a descriptive name in the Digital ID Name box.
  5. Click OK.

docs.title: Step 5: Set Security Level (if prompted)
docs.text: If prompted, click OK to set the security level.

docs.title: Step 6: Configure the Certificate for Use in Outlook
docs.text:
1. Click Settings to configure Outlook to use the certificate.
  2. Enter a name in the Security Settings Name field (choose something meaningful to you).
  3. Click the top Choose button, then click OK on the Select a Certificate window. The newly imported certificate should be the only option. If multiple certificates appear, select the DigiCert certificate dated the day you downloaded it.
  4. Change the Hash Algorithm to SHA256.
  5. Click OK to complete the configuration.
  6. Click OK again to close the Trust Center.

docs.title: How to Share SharePoint Files from File Manager
docs.text: This guide explains how to share files and folders stored in SharePoint directly from Windows File Explorer.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Your SharePoint files must already be synced and visible in File Explorer via OneDrive.
  • You must be signed in with your company account.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open File Explorer on your taskbar.

  2. In File Explorer, find the file or folder you want to share. Then, right-click on it. A drop-down menu will appear. Click Share.

  3. The sharing pop-up will appear. Click on the "People you specify can view" button (wording may vary based on your setup).

  4. Choose your sharing settings (your company name will appear in place of "Imperitiv"):

  5. Anyone with the link — Allows anyone who has the link to access it.

  6. People in "[Your Company Name]" with the link — Allows anyone within your company who has signed in with their company account to access it.
  7. People with existing access — Allows anyone who already has access to the whole site to access it. Use this option when you want to direct someone to a file or folder without granting new access.

  8. Specific people — Only people you choose can access it. Type their full email address for external users; for internal users, type enough of their name for it to auto-populate.

  9. You can also check or uncheck the "Allow editing" option to provide read-only or edit access. Note: if you allow editing, you cannot block the download at this level.

  10. When finished, click Apply.

  11. If you wish to send a message, click in the "Message…" area and type your message, then click Send.

Important: The email sent via this method may appear as spam to the recipient. It is recommended that you instead open your own email account (Outlook), create a new email, and paste the link manually.

  1. To copy the link, click the Copy button. A new window will appear — click the new Copy button and paste the link into an email you compose manually in Outlook. Once sent, you have completed the process.

docs.title: Computer Performance — NIC Latency Improvements
docs.text:
To improve latency on a NIC:

  1. Update NIC Drivers — Updated drivers can include performance improvements and bug fixes.
  2. Enable Jumbo Frames — Increases data transmission efficiency (more beneficial for throughput than latency).
  3. Disable Interrupt Moderation — Reduces CPU load-induced latency at the cost of higher CPU usage.
  4. Adjust Receive and Transmit Buffers — Increasing buffer size can improve performance, but setting too high can have adverse effects.
  5. Enable Flow Control — Helps control data rate, preventing packet loss during high traffic.
  6. Disable Energy Efficient Ethernet (EEE) — Prevents the NIC from having to "wake up" from a low-power state.
  7. Disable Green Ethernet — Similar to EEE; disabling can provide a slight latency improvement.
  8. Optimize for Performance over Power Saving — Select performance priority in the NIC's advanced properties.
  9. Tweak TCP/IP Settings — Via Windows Registry or third-party utilities (proceed with caution).
  10. Use Wired Connections — Wired Ethernet is generally faster and more stable than Wi-Fi.

docs.title: MFA: Windows Hello + Authenticator vs. Windows Hello + Duo
Trust & Integration
docs.text:
• Hello + Authenticator (Microsoft native): Full native integration with Windows sign-in, Azure AD, Conditional Access, and Intune. Supports phishing-resistant MFA with number matching. Enables passwordless sign-in via FIDO2 or Hello for Business.
  • Hello + Duo: Handles the second factor but can't natively enforce all Conditional Access conditions. Works well for non-Microsoft apps, legacy VPNs, and RDP gateways.

docs.title: Security Strength Comparison
docs.text: Factor Hello + Authenticator Hello + Duo Phishing Resistance High with passwordless/number matching High with Duo push and number matching Policy Enforcement Unified in Azure AD Conditional Access Split between Azure AD and Duo User Experience Seamless, passwordless, SSO across Microsoft Slightly more friction, may prompt separately Legacy/Non-Microsoft Apps Requires custom integration Integrates more easily with legacy apps & VPNs

docs.title: When Authenticator Wins
docs.text:
• All-in on Microsoft 365/Azure AD/Intune.
  • Want passwordless sign-in with phishing-resistant MFA built into Windows Hello.
  • Fewer moving parts and a single policy engine.
  • Native protection for OAuth and WS-Fed.

docs.title: When Duo Still Adds Value
docs.text:
• Many non-Microsoft apps not integrated with Azure AD.
  • Need MFA for VPNs, SSH, or RDP that can't use Azure MFA natively.
  • Need MFA logging/reporting independent of Microsoft.
  • Compliance requirements specify a separate MFA provider.

Bottom line: If your environment is primarily Microsoft-based, Windows Hello + Conditional Access + Microsoft Authenticator is usually more secure and streamlined. Duo makes the most sense for cross-platform MFA or layered defense requirements.

docs.title: Mapping Azure Files with Azure AD (No AAD DS)
docs.text:
Use Microsoft Entra Kerberos for hybrid identities to authenticate users against Azure AD:

  1. Enable Microsoft Entra Kerberos Authentication on the storage account.
  2. Assign Share-Level Permissions via Azure RBAC (roles: Storage File Data SMB Share Reader/Contributor/Elevated Contributor).
  3. Configure Directory and File-Level Permissions using Windows ACLs.

Note: Entra Kerberos supports hybrid user identities only — users must exist in both on-premises AD and Azure AD.

When to use AAD DS vs. Entra Kerberos: - Use AAD DS if: cloud-only identities, need granular NTFS folder-level permissions. - Use Entra Kerberos if: hybrid environment with on-prem AD, share-level permissions are sufficient.

docs.title: OneDrive Stuck on "Processing Changes" / Office Apps Crashing
docs.text:
If OneDrive is stuck on "Processing changes" without showing files being actively synced, or Office apps crash/freeze when opening documents:

  1. Check if Microsoft Office Click-to-Run is taking up more than ~50MB of RAM in Task Manager. If so, proceed.
  2. Open Services.msc, find the Microsoft Click-to-Run Service, and set startup behavior to Disabled.
  3. Reboot.
  4. Open C:\Users\Username\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Office\Spw and delete all files and folders within.
  5. Open C:\Users\Username\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Office\15.0\OfficeFileCache and delete all files and folders within.
  6. Re-open Services.msc, find Microsoft Click-to-Run Service, and set startup back to Automatic.
  7. Reboot.
  8. Verify that OneDrive now shows "Your files are synced" or shows a list of actively syncing files.
  9. Verify in Task Manager that Microsoft Office Click-to-Run is taking no more than ~50MB of RAM.

Note: Check the OneDrive DAT file — it has a 2 GB size limit in older versions. Performance degrades when the file exceeds ~200 MB. Path: %localappdata%\Microsoft\OneDrive\settings\Business1

docs.title: Machine Not in MEM — Add a Windows Computer to MEM Manually
docs.text:
Check for the registry key HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\MDM with DWORD values: - AutoEnrollMDM = 1 - UseAADCredentialType = 1

Create via PowerShell:

$registryPath = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\MDM"
New-Item -Path $registryPath
$Name = "AutoEnrollMDM"
$Name2 = "UseAADCredentialType"
$value = "1"
New-ItemProperty -Path $registryPath -Name $Name -Value $value -PropertyType DWORD -Force | Out-Null
New-ItemProperty -Path $registryPath -Name $Name2 -Value $value -PropertyType DWORD -Force | Out-Null
gpupdate /force

After creation, run (using PsExec):

C:\Windows\system32\deviceenroller.exe /c /AutoEnrollMDM

docs.title: MAM Instead of MDM
docs.text:
$EnrollmentsPath = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments\"
$Enrollments = Get-ChildItem -Path $EnrollmentsPath
$DiscoveryServerFullUrls = @("https://wip.mam.manage.microsoft.com/Enroll")
Foreach ($Enrollment in $Enrollments) {
    $EnrollmentObject = Get-ItemProperty Registry::$Enrollment
    if ($EnrollmentObject."DiscoveryServiceFullURL" -in $DiscoveryServerFullUrls) {
        $EnrollmentPath = $EnrollmentsPath + $EnrollmentObject."PSChildName"
        Remove-Item -Path $EnrollmentPath -Recurse
        & "C:\Windows\System32\deviceenroller.exe /c /AutoEnrollMDM"
    }
}

docs.title: Error 80190026 — Previously Enrolled in SCCM
docs.text: Open: SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments Ensure the ExternallyManaged key doesn't exist or is set to 0, not 1.

docs.title: Error 0x80180026
docs.text: Check the Devicemanagement-Enterprise-Diagnostics-Provider event log. Ensure Disable MDM Enrollment policy is not preventing enrollment.

docs.title: Error 0x8018002a / 0x80070003
docs.text: Likely caused by Conditional Access rules requiring MFA for "All Cloud Apps". Exclude the Microsoft Intune Enrollment Cloud app from the Conditional Access rule.

docs.title: Error 0x8018002b
docs.text:
Possible causes: - UPN Issues — Ensure the user's UPN matches your routable domain. - MDM Scope — Configure MDM scope to "All" or include the user in the scoped group. - User License — Verify the user is licensed for Intune. - Patience — Enrollment can take time; wait before retrying. - Previously AADR Enrolled — Remove lingering enrollments via PowerShell:

$RegistryKeys = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments\Status",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\EnterpriseResourceManager\Tracked",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PolicyManager\AdmxInstalled",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PolicyManager\Providers",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\OMADM\Accounts",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\OMADM\Logger",
                "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\OMADM\Sessions"

$IntuneCert = Get-ChildItem -Path Cert:\LocalMachine\My |
    Where-Object { $_.Issuer -match "Intune MDM" } | Remove-Item

$EnrollmentID = Get-ScheduledTask |
    Where-Object { $_.TaskPath -like "*Microsoft*Windows*EnterpriseMgmt*" } |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty TaskPath -Unique |
    Where-Object { $_ -like "*-*-*" } | Split-Path -Leaf

Get-ScheduledTask |
    Where-Object { $_.Taskpath -match $EnrollmentID } |
    Unregister-ScheduledTask -Confirm:$false

foreach ($Key in $RegistryKeys) {
    if (Test-Path -Path $Key) {
        Get-ChildItem -Path $Key |
            Where-Object { $_.Name -match $EnrollmentID } |
            Remove-Item -Recurse -Force -Confirm:$false -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
    }
}

Start-Sleep -Seconds 30
$EnrollmentProcess = Start-Process -FilePath "C:\Windows\System32\DeviceEnroller.exe" `

docs.title: Schedule to Enroll in MDM from AAD Not Created
docs.text:
Manually create the scheduled task:

$TaskScheduleName = "Schedule created by enrollment client for automatically enrolling in MDM from AAD"
$Date = Get-Date -Format "yyyy-MM-dd"
$Time = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(5).ToString("HH:mm:ss")
$task = @"
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-16"?>
<Task version="1.3" xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/windows/2004/02/mit/task">
  <RegistrationInfo>
    <Author>Microsoft Corporation</Author>
    <URI>\Microsoft\Windows\EnterpriseMgmt\Schedule created by enrollment client for automatically enrolling in MDM from AAD</URI>
  </RegistrationInfo>
  <Triggers>
    <TimeTrigger>
      <Repetition>
        <Interval>PT5M</Interval>
        <Duration>P1D</Duration>
        <StopAtDurationEnd>true</StopAtDurationEnd>
      </Repetition>
      <StartBoundary>$($Date)T$($Time)</StartBoundary>
      <Enabled>true</Enabled>
    </TimeTrigger>
  </Triggers>
  <Principals>
    <Principal id="Author">
      <UserId>S-1-5-18</UserId>
      <RunLevel>LeastPrivilege</RunLevel>
    </Principal>
  </Principals>
  <Settings>
    <MultipleInstancesPolicy>Queue</MultipleInstancesPolicy>
    <DisallowStartIfOnBatteries>false</DisallowStartIfOnBatteries>
    <StopIfGoingOnBatteries>false</StopIfGoingOnBatteries>
    <AllowHardTerminate>true</AllowHardTerminate>
    <StartWhenAvailable>true</StartWhenAvailable>
    <RunOnlyIfNetworkAvailable>true</RunOnlyIfNetworkAvailable>
    <AllowStartOnDemand>true</AllowStartOnDemand>
    <Enabled>true</Enabled>
    <ExecutionTimeLimit>PT1H</ExecutionTimeLimit>
    <Priority>7</Priority>
  </Settings>
  <Actions Context="Author">
    <Exec>
      <Command>%windir%\system32\deviceenroller.exe</Command>
      <Arguments>/c /AutoEnrollMDM</Arguments>
    </Exec>
  </Actions>
</Task>
"@
(Register-ScheduledTask -XML $task -TaskName $TaskScheduleName -Force) | Out-Null

docs.title: Ubiquiti Networks Deployment Process
docs.text: A reference guide for deploying and configuring Ubiquiti UniFi network infrastructure.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use proper templated names for primary externally-visible devices such as UDMs, UDM Pros, UDRs, Dream Walls, USGs, or Enterprise Gateways:

[COMPANY CODE]-[DEVICE CODE]-[TWO LETTER STATE ABBREVIATION]-[CITY ABBREVIATION]

docs.title: Valid Device Codes
docs.text:
Code Device UDM Dream Machine UDMPRO Dream Machine Pro UDMSE Dream Machine SE UDR Dream Router UDRULTRA Dream Router Ultra UDW Dream Wall UCK Cloud Key UCKG2 Cloud Key G2 Plus USG Secure Gateway USGPRO Secure Gateway Professional UCKENT Cloud Key Enterprise UCC UniFi Cloud Console

Note: For cloud consoles, use [UCC]-[COMPANYNAME] — these consoles don't have a specific location.

Remaining UniFi devices should be named appropriately for their location or function. The primary root switch should be named "Core Switch". APs should be labeled with a specific location (e.g., "Conference Room AP" or "Room 3118 AP").

docs.title: Wi-Fi Networks
docs.text:
• Set up the primary Wi-Fi network using WPA3 only and all bands.
  • Set up the guest network (if requested) using WPA2 or WPA2/3 hybrid mode, all APs, and the 2.4/5GHz bands only. The 6 GHz performance band should be limited to the primary network only.

docs.title: Guest Network Configuration
docs.text:
1. Create a speed limit profile under Settings → Profiles → Wi-Fi speed limit. Generally, limit to 25% each of the maximum upload and download bandwidth of the incoming WAN connection.
  2. Configure a captive portal (if requested): enable HTTPS, enforce branding, enforce terms of service, and redirect the success landing page to the client's main webpage.
  3. Enforce speed limits: Settings → WiFi → [guest network name] → WiFi Speed Limit.
  4. Under Settings → WiFi → [guest network name] → WiFi Scheduler, create a time schedule to turn off guest WiFi outside of business hours. Typical guideline: shut off from 20:00 to 04:00 daily.

docs.title: Security Settings
docs.text:
• Settings → Teleport and VPN → Teleport tab: Turn off Teleport VPN. (The Teleport app only works on iOS, Android, and macOS.)
  • Settings → Application Firewall:
  • Set Suspicious Activity toggle to Notify and Block.
  • Set Detection Sensitivity to High.
  • Enable Dark Web Blocker and Malicious Website Blocker.
  • Settings → Application Firewall → Gateway Engine:
  • Turn on Device and Traffic Identification.
  • Turn on Country Restrictions and block all countries in the Country Block List (see below).

docs.title: Updates and Backups
docs.text:
• Settings → System → Updates: Uncheck "Automate Device Updates." Add a new device update schedule repeating monthly at 00:00 hours. Select all applicable devices.
  • Settings → System → Backups: Click "Console Cloud Backups" and ensure System Config Backup is checked with a recent backup.

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text:
• Settings → System → Advanced: Ensure WiFiman Support is checked.
  • Under Wireless Connectivity: Disable Wireless Meshing if all APs are hardwired to the controller. Leave enabled if using wireless UniFi devices (e.g., wireless G4 Doorbell, UniFi Protect wireless cameras).

docs.title: Country Block List
docs.text:
Block the following countries in the Application Firewall:

Afghanistan, Azerbaijan, Bangladesh, Bahrain, Brazil, Belarus, Congo (DRC), Central African Republic, Congo (ROC), China, Colombia, Cuba, Egypt, Hong Kong, Honduras, Indonesia, India, Iraq, Iran, Jordan, Kyrgyzstan, North Korea, Kazakhstan, Sri Lanka, Libya, Myanmar, Mongolia, Nigeria, Oman, Philippines, Pakistan, Qatar, Russia, Rwanda, Saudi Arabia, Sudan, Somalia, South Africa, Syria, Thailand, Turkmenistan, Turkey, United Arab Emirates, Uzbekistan, Venezuela, Vietnam, Yemen, Zimbabwe

docs.title: Switches
docs.text:
• Set switch priorities in UniFi Devices → [Switch] → Settings tab.
  • Proper switching architecture: the main router should only be connected to the core switch. Do not connect any devices directly to the UDM/UDR/UDW except the primary core switch.
  • Set the core switch RSTP priority to 4096 in Switch Settings → Advanced. This should be the ONLY switch assigned this priority at each site.
  • Secondary switches (directly connected to core): 8192
  • Tertiary switches (connected to secondary): 12288
  • And so on.

Note: For setups where a higher-level switch may be added later (e.g., aggregation/leaf switch), start the core switch at 8192 and reserve 4096 for the future aggregator.

  • Any core switch connected directly to a UniFi Gateway should use the highest-bandwidth port available. For advanced switches, use a 1G or 10G SFP-to-SFP "direct attach cable" to offload routing to the SFP backplane.
  • Example cable: https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09P517YVM/

docs.title: Access Points
docs.text:
1. Update all APs to the latest firmware version.
  2. If all APs are hardwired, disable meshing in device settings for each AP (this is per-AP, not in Global AP Settings).
  3. Under Settings → Global AP Settings, set transmit power to Medium for all radios (adjustable after installation).
  4. Channel width settings:
  5. 2.4 GHz band: Always set to the lowest channel width to maximize coverage on the fallback radio.
  6. 5.0 GHz band: Set to 40 MHz. If uninterrupted line of sight to >80% of client devices, can be set to 80 MHz.
  7. 6 GHz band: Set to 160 MHz to maximize bandwidth for supported clients within range.

docs.title: WAN Failover Devices (U-LTE)
docs.text:
1. Raise the data limit to 5GB/10GB in device settings (default shuts off after 3GB).
  2. Enable external antenna support in device settings only after connecting the external RP-SMA antenna.
  3. Connect the U-LTE to the backup WAN port. If the primary WAN port uses RJ45, you'll need a 1G or 10G/1G auto-negotiating SFP+ to RJ45 module:
  4. UACC-CM-RJ45-1G from Ubiquiti Store

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text: The purpose of this policy is to assure that systems containing PII and/or sensitive company data are accessed only by those persons or software programs that have been granted appropriate access rights.

docs.title: Unique User Identification
docs.text:
• Employees will be assigned a unique user identification (user ID) to access any system or application that transmits, receives, or stores PII and/or sensitive company data.
  • Each employee must ensure their assigned user ID is appropriately protected and only used for legitimate access.
  • If an employee believes their user ID has been compromised, they must report the security incident immediately.

docs.title: Password Requirements
docs.text:
Employees should be aware of the following procedures to create strong passwords:

  • Minimum of eight (8) characters in length.
  • Must incorporate both upper and lower case letters (a–z and A–Z).
  • Must incorporate digits and punctuation characters (0–9, `! @ # $ % ^ & * ( ) _ - + = { } [ ] : ; " ' | \ / ? < > , . ~ ``).
  • Must not be words found in a dictionary.
  • Must not include easily guessed personal information (names, pets, birth dates, etc.).

docs.title: Password Protection Procedures
docs.text:
• Passwords must not be written down.
  • Passwords must not be shared with other employees.
  • If an employee suspects their password has been compromised, they must report the incident immediately.
  • Passwords must be changed at least every 90 days, unless Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is in use and enforced.
  • After a number of failed password attempts, the employee's account will be disabled.

docs.title: Automatic Logoff
docs.text:
• Systems accessing or storing PII and/or sensitive company data should implement automatic logoff after a determined period of inactivity (e.g., 10 minutes).
  • When leaving a workstation, server, or other computer system unattended, employees must lock the system (e.g., CTRL + ALT + DELETE > Lock) or log out of all applications and systems containing PII.

docs.title: Encryption and Decryption
docs.text:
• To the extent technically feasible, all portable devices containing PII and/or sensitive company data should be encrypted.
  • Encryption should be used when sending any PII across public networks and wireless networks, including email and the Internet.
  • Employees should be trained on the use of encryption to protect PII and sensitive company data.
  • All backup tapes and media containing PII and/or sensitive company data should utilize encryption.
  • Secure encrypted remote access procedures should be implemented for systems accessing or storing PII. Examples include VPN clients, authenticated SSL web sessions, and encrypted Citrix/RDP client access.
  • Two-factor authentication (MFA) (e.g., SMS PIN notification) should be implemented where technically feasible.
  • All wireless access to networks should utilize encryption mechanisms.
  • Employees should not utilize open public Wi-Fi networks.

docs.title: Security Policy #9: Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) Policy
Purpose of Policy
docs.text: The purpose of this policy is to develop appropriate safeguards to protect PII and sensitive company data on employee personally owned devices. Proper security controls are essential to protect sensitive information on these devices. Documented instructions and requirements must be provided to all employees who may access or store PII and sensitive company data on personally owned devices, and acknowledgement of acceptance must be documented and retained.

docs.title: Policy
docs.text:
• The Company may be responsible for breaches of PII and may suffer consequences of breached sensitive company data resulting from unsecured employee personally owned devices.
  • Employees must be aware that breaches or inappropriate use of their devices that put PII and/or sensitive company data at risk may negatively affect customers, The Company, and the employee themselves. The Company has the right to revoke access to PII and/or sensitive company data or levy sanctions per the sanction policy.
  • Encryption usually offers a safe harbor under federal and state regulations and is the strongest protection against a data breach. Encryption should be used on all devices that access or store PII and/or sensitive company data.
  • Employees are not permitted to access PII and/or sensitive company data on personally owned devices unless authorized and approved. Only approved and properly configured devices will be granted access.
  • The Company will limit who has access to PII on personally owned devices, providing only the minimum access necessary to perform job functions.
  • The organization and their IT group/provider will work together to manage and enforce this policy.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
• Employees must request permission to use personally owned devices and complete the registration form.
  • The Company and IT will periodically review and update this policy when new requirements are implemented or security requirements change. Employees must be notified of changes and provide signed acknowledgement.
  • The Company and IT reserve the right to monitor and inspect registered devices to ensure PII and sensitive company data are being properly protected.
  • Upon an employee's termination, The Company and IT will ensure that any devices with PII and/or sensitive company data are returned to IT for final analysis and removal of PII/sensitive data or applications that access such data. This will be conducted as soon as possible.
  • Documentation, acknowledgement, and registration forms will be retained in the employee's personnel folder.

docs.title: Devices Permitted
docs.text:
• Smartphones accepted (brand and model): _____
  • Tablets accepted (brand and model): _________
  • Laptops: Personally owned laptops must be accepted and approved by management.
  • Additional Devices: Must be approved by management and IT.
  • Specifically excluded devices: ________

docs.title: Security Specifications
docs.text: Setting Value Mobile Device Management service Encryption service Anti-Malware/Anti-Virus service Minimum OS required (laptop) Minimum OS required (smartphones) Minimum OS required (tablets) Secure texting application required System Inactivity timeout setting (minutes) Email Encryption provider

docs.title: Security Requirements
docs.text:
• All devices must be password protected.
  • Passwords must be complex — minimum 6 characters, combination of upper- and lower-case letters, numbers, and symbols.
  • Devices must lock after five (5) incorrect password attempts.
  • Devices must "time out" and require a password after 5 minutes of inactivity.
  • Text messages containing PII and/or sensitive company data must be sent through the secure texting application provided. If no secure texting app is provided, employees should not send PII via text.
  • Emails containing PII and/or sensitive company data must be sent encrypted. If secure email encryption is not provided, employees should not send PII via email.

docs.title: Restrictions and Limitations
docs.text:
• "Rooted" or "Jailbroken" devices are not permitted to access PII and/or sensitive company data.
  • Employees must notify management when selling, trading in, recycling, or disposing of their personal devices.
  • The employee's device may have data remotely deleted/wiped if:
  • The device is lost or stolen.
  • The employee terminates their employment.
  • IT detects a data or policy breach, virus, or similar threat.
  • Lost or stolen devices must be reported to management and/or IT within 24 hours.
  • Employees must inform management and/or IT if they plan to upgrade, recycle, or dispose of their device.
  • Employees who voluntarily resign must present their device(s) to IT within 48 hours to have all PII and sensitive company data removed.
  • Employees who do not turn over their device within 48 hours after voluntary resignation are subject to a full remote wipe of all data on their device.
  • For scheduled terminations: Terminated employees must present their device(s) to IT on the day of termination. Employees who fail to do so, after being given the opportunity, are subject to a full remote wipe.

docs.title: Sanctions
docs.text: Violations or abuse of this policy are subject to the repercussions laid out in The Company's sanction policy.

docs.title: Device Registration Form
docs.text:
Field Value Employee name Position/title Phone number Secondary phone number Device and description Serial Number MAC Address Access points (where will company information be accessed — email, text, apps, web, etc.)

I have read, understand, and agree to comply with the foregoing policies, rules, and conditions governing the use of personally owned devices that may access, store, or transmit PII and sensitive company data. I am aware that violations may subject me to retraction of this privilege or disciplinary action, including termination of employment. I understand that The Company reserves the right to remotely wipe/delete data from my device if the need arises.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text: The purpose of this policy is to ensure that employees understand what functions should and should not be performed on The Company's computers and network to maximize the security of PII and sensitive company data. The policy also provides guidance regarding proper safeguards when accessing social media sites.

docs.title: Computer Use Policy
docs.text:
• The Company may provide workstations and other computer systems to employees for the purpose of performing their job functions. Employees shall be responsible for using workstations appropriately in conformance with this Policy.
  • The Company may remove or deactivate any employee's user privileges — including user access accounts and access to secured areas — when necessary to preserve the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of its facilities, user services, and data.
  • Employees must be assigned and use a unique User ID and Password. See Security Policy #6 – Access Control.
  • Employees using The Company's information systems and workstation assets should have no expectation of privacy. The Company may log, review, or monitor any data stored or transmitted on its information system assets.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Computers, the Internet, and electronic mail (email) are powerful research, communication, and time-saving tools available to employees. Like any tools, they have the potential to be used for inappropriate purposes.

docs.title: Policy
docs.text:
The following policies on computer, Internet, and email usage shall be observed by all employees:

  • Users must comply with all appropriate laws, regulations, and generally accepted Internet etiquette.
  • The primary purpose of the Internet and email is to conduct official business.
  • Users should identify themselves properly, conduct themselves professionally, and be aware that their activities reflect on the reputation and integrity of the company.
  • Each user is individually responsible for the content of any communication sent over or placed on the Internet and email.
  • All employees have a responsibility to ensure a respectful workplace. Computer equipment must not be used to visit Internet sites that contain pornographic or sexually explicit information, pictures, or cartoons.
  • Exceptions are only allowed when pre-approved by supervisors or company management for official business, research, or investigatory work.

docs.title: Prohibited Actions
docs.text:
The following actions are prohibited. It is unacceptable for employees to:

  1. Knowingly or intentionally publish, display, transmit, retrieve, or store inappropriate or offensive material on any company computer system.
  2. Create or distribute defamatory, false, inaccurate, abusive, threatening, racially offensive, or otherwise biased, discriminatory, or illegal material.
  3. View or distribute obscene, pornographic, profane, or sexually oriented material.
  4. Violate laws, rules, and regulations prohibiting sexual harassment.
  5. Engage in any unauthorized activities for personal financial gain.
  6. Place advertisements for commercial enterprises.
  7. Download, disseminate, store, or print materials including articles and software in violation of copyright laws.
  8. Download any software (including games, screen savers, toolbars, or browsing tools) without the permission of supervisors, company management, or IT staff.
  9. Violate or infringe on the rights of others.
  10. Conduct business unauthorized by the company.
  11. Restrict or inhibit other users from using the system or reduce computer system efficiency.
  12. Cause congestion or disruption of networks or systems, including distribution of chain letters.
  13. Transmit incendiary statements or promote the use of weapons.
  14. Use the system for any illegal purpose or contrary to company policy or business interests.
  15. Connect a personal computer to the company network without having it checked by IT staff.
  16. Monitor or intercept the files or electronic communications of other employees or third parties.
  17. Hack or obtain access to systems or accounts they are not authorized to use.
  18. Disclose a Login ID or password to anyone, or allow anyone to access any information system with someone else's credentials.
  19. Use other people's Login IDs or passwords to access any information system for any reason.
  20. Post any PII or sensitive company data on social network sites or public forums, including pictures of PII or customers without permission.
  21. Remove electronic media containing PII or confidential/proprietary information unless authorized by a supervisor or company management.

docs.title: Acknowledgement
docs.text:
Any employee who abuses the privilege of access to email or the Internet in violation of this policy will be subject to corrective action, including possible termination of employment, legal action, and criminal liability.

By signing the Computer Use Policy, I agree to comply with its terms and conditions. Failure to read this Agreement is not an excuse for violating it. The Company may deny access to information systems if this Agreement is not returned signed and dated.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text: All media containing PII and sensitive company data will be disposed of in a manner that destroys the data and does not allow unauthorized access.

docs.title: Procedures for Computer/Hardware Disposal
docs.text:
1. The Security Officer or delegate will notify IT of equipment that needs to be disposed of.
  2. The Security Officer or delegate will determine the data sensitivity of the data to be disposed of (see Data Classification Table below).
  3. IT will assess the condition of the equipment and:
  4. Track the disposal of the device (type of hardware, serial number, etc.) — See Appendix A: Media Disposal Log.
  5. Run approved wiping software on all devices to ensure all PII and sensitive company data is removed.
  6. This may include physical destruction (see Methods of Destruction below).
  7. Verify that the hardware's data has been removed.
  8. Dispose of the hardware.
  9. The Security Officer or delegate/IT will document the destruction and retain the record — See Appendix A: Media Disposal Log.
  10. If taken to an outside facility — The media shall be taken to an approved, certified facility for erasure or destruction. A letter of certification regarding the date and time of erasure/destruction shall be obtained.

docs.title: Data Classification Table
docs.text: Classification Description Disposal Requirement Low (Unclassified) Basic operating system, personal files, etc. No requirement to erase, but normally erase using any means such as reformatting or degaussing. Medium (Sensitive but not Confidential) Business-related information not considered sensitive company data. Erase using any means such as reformatting or degaussing. High (Confidential) PII and sensitive company data. Data must be erased using an approved technology to ensure it cannot be read using special forensic techniques.

docs.title: Examples of Hardware Devices Covered
docs.text:
• Workstations and laptops
  • Tablets (iPad/Android)
  • Smartphones
  • Server hard drives
  • Memory sticks (USB drives)
  • CD-ROM/DVD-ROM discs
  • Storage/Backup tapes
  • Hard drives
  • Copiers/Scanners/Fax machines
  • Any equipment that contains PII or sensitive company data

docs.title: Methods of Destruction
docs.text: Method Description Clear Use software or hardware products to overwrite storage space with non-sensitive data. This process replaces written data with random data. Cannot be used for media that is damaged or not rewriteable. Purge Degaussing or executing the firmware Secure Erase command (for ATA drives only). Degaussing exposes magnetic media to a strong magnetic field to disrupt recorded magnetic domains. Effective for damaged, inoperative, or large-capacity media. Destroy Physical destruction of media to render it completely unreadable and unrecoverable.

docs.title: Appendix A — Media Disposal Log
docs.text:
All disposed/destroyed media must be logged. The record should include:

  • Date of Destruction
  • Authorized By
  • Description of Information Disposed of or Destroyed (Manufacturer/Model/Serial Number/etc.)
  • Backup of PII or sensitive company data? (Required if this is the only copy) — Yes / No / If Yes, list backup location
  • Method of Destruction: Clear / Purge / Destroy
  • Destruction Method Used
  • Final Disposition of Media: Disposed / Reused Internally / Reused Externally (sold/donated/etc.) / Returned to Manufacturer/Leasing Company/Vendor / Other

Save this log and retain indefinitely. Upload to the Security Portal.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text: The purpose of this policy is to define the procedures that will limit physical access to PII and sensitive company data and the facility or facilities in which such systems are housed, while still ensuring that proper authorized access is allowed.

docs.title: Facility Security Requirements
docs.text:
• Physical security of office buildings must be implemented to protect PII and sensitive data as well as other company assets. Physical measures may include: alarm systems, surveillance cameras, fences, locked gates/doors, etc.

  • All systems that store or access PII and/or sensitive company data should be stored in locked rooms, closets, or cabinets to prevent unauthorized access. Access to these facilities should be minimized and limited only to employees and/or vendors who need access to perform their job function.

  • Where practical, all visitors should be restricted from areas where files or systems containing PII and/or sensitive company data are stored. Alternatively, visitors must be escorted or accompanied by an approved employee in any area where such files or systems are stored.

  • A clean desk policy will be implemented and includes the following:

  • All employees are prohibited from keeping unsecured paper files containing PII and sensitive company data in their work area when they are not present (e.g., during lunch breaks).
  • At the end of the day, all files containing PII and/or sensitive company data must be stored in a locked filing cabinet, desk drawer, or other locked location.

  • Any systems that store or access PII and/or sensitive company data should be closed or logged off when unattended.

  • The Security Officer shall maintain a secured and confidential master list of all lock combinations, passcodes, and keys. This list will identify which employees possess keys, keycards, or other access devices and confirm that only approved employees have been provided access credentials.

  • Where practical, all visitors expected to access areas other than common space or areas containing PII and/or sensitive company data should be required to sign in with a Photo ID at a designated reception area, where they will be assigned a visitor's ID or guest badge, unless escorted at all times.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text:
The purpose of this policy is to develop the response to and reporting of security incidents, including:

  • The identification of and response to suspected or known security incidents.
  • The mitigation of the harmful effects of known security incidents, to the extent possible.
  • The documentation of security incidents and their outcomes.

Note: Breach definitions, remediation steps, and breach notification requirements vary between federal regulations (HIPAA, GLBA, etc.) and between individual states. It is highly recommended to consult with breach experts or legal counsel to determine The Company's responsibilities.

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
Breach — The acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of personally identifiable information (PII) or sensitive company data (such as email, employee information, confidential information, etc.) which compromises the security or privacy of the PII or sensitive company data.

Unsecured PII — PII that is not rendered unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable to unauthorized individuals through the use of a technology or methodology such as encryption. The definition of unsecured PII varies between different federal and state regulations.

docs.title: Reporting and Response
docs.text:
• The Company will ensure that all incidents, threats, or violations that affect or may affect the privacy, confidentiality, integrity, or availability of PII and sensitive company data will be reported and responded to.
  • The Company shall have a Security Incident Response Team (SIRT) charged with identifying, evaluating, and responding to security incidents. The Privacy Security Officer shall oversee SIRT activities.
  • The SIRT will investigate all known or suspected privacy and security incidents.
  • The SIRT will document a procedure for all employees to follow to report incidents (see Appendix A – Security Incident Response Log).
  • All employees must receive training on how to identify and report security incidents.
  • All employees must follow the documented procedure to report incidents and must report all known or suspected security incidents.
  • All employees must assist the SIRT with security incident investigations.

docs.title: Breach Determination
docs.text: The SIRT will investigate all reported and suspected security breaches and refer to applicable federal and state regulations to help with breach determination. Breach determination varies significantly between regulations and between states.

docs.title: Breach Notification
docs.text: If the SIRT determines that a breach of unsecured PII has occurred, breach notification of affected individuals may be required.

docs.title: Key Elements of a Breach Notification
docs.text:
Date of Discovery — Usually the first day the breach is known, or would have been known with reasonable diligence.

Timeliness of Notification — The Company will provide required notifications without unreasonable delay. The timeframe varies by regulation.

Content of Notification — If required, notifications must include: - A brief description of what happened, including the date of breach and date of discovery. - A description of the types of unsecured PII involved. - Steps individuals should take to protect themselves from potential harm. - A description of what The Company is doing to investigate the breach, mitigate harm, and prevent further breaches. - Contact procedures for individuals to ask questions or learn additional information (telephone number, email address, website, or postal address).

The notification should be written in plain language.

docs.title: Methods of Notification
docs.text:
Written Notice — First-class mail to the individual's last known address, or via email if the individual has agreed to email notice. If the individual is deceased, notification is usually sent to next of kin or a personal representative.

Substitute Notice — If contact information is out of date, a substitute notification may be used, typically a conspicuous posting on The Company's website home page or conspicuous notice in major print or broadcast media in areas where affected individuals likely reside. The notice should include a contact phone number.

Notification to Media — In addition to notifying individuals, notification to the media may be required.

Notification to Federal or State Regulatory Agencies — The Company may need to report breaches to applicable regulatory agencies.

Notification by Third Party Service Providers — Third Party Service Providers responsible for a breach of The Company's PII should be required to notify The Company within a pre-determined timeframe as defined in the Service Provider Agreement.

docs.title: Appendix A — Security Incident Response Log
docs.text: All security incidents should be documented in the Security Incident Response Log or the Security Incidents Module in the Security Portal.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text: The purpose of this policy is to describe the physical safeguards applicable for each server, desktop computer system, and wireless computer system used to access, transmit, receive, and store PII and sensitive company data to ensure that appropriate security is maintained and that access is restricted to authorized employees.

docs.title: Network Security Requirements
docs.text:
The Company will take reasonable and appropriate steps to prevent unauthorized access to workstations, servers, and portable devices (including laptops, smartphones, CD-ROMs, DVDs, USB drives, etc.) that store or access PII and sensitive company data.

  • Workstations and laptops in common areas should be physically placed so that unauthorized people cannot view confidential information such as logins, passwords, PII, and sensitive company data.
  • Workstations and laptops in common areas should utilize privacy screens to prevent unauthorized viewing.
  • Workstations and laptops in common areas should be secured with locking cables or similar restraints.
  • To the extent technically feasible, all portable devices containing PII and/or sensitive company data should be encrypted. Encryption should also be used when sending PII across public networks, wireless networks, email, and the Internet.
  • Portable devices and media should be concealed from view when offsite to prevent theft.
  • All network servers, application servers, routers, database systems, device management hardware, and other servers should be located in a physically secured room or area, accessible only to authorized personnel via lock and key or other appropriate security mechanism.
  • All workstations, servers, and portable devices must run anti-virus/anti-malware software with current and up-to-date virus/malware definitions. Employees must not disable these tools unless specifically directed by IT support.
  • A network firewall should be in place and up to date. Firewalls should be monitored and alerts should trigger in the event of unauthorized or suspected intrusion.
  • Log files from network equipment (firewalls, servers, desktops, laptops, and other devices) should be stored and retained. Retention length may vary depending on federal, state, or industry regulations.
  • All workstations, servers, and portable devices must implement a security patch and update procedure to ensure all relevant patches are promptly applied based on vulnerability severity.
  • Periodic network vulnerability scans should be performed on all internal and external (internet-facing) systems. Known vulnerabilities should be remediated. After remediation, an external penetration test should be performed to confirm unauthorized external access is prevented.
  • Workstations must not be located where they will be directly affected by extremes of temperature or electromagnetic interference. Precautions should also be taken to protect against utility-related threats (water, sewer, steam lines, etc.).
  • All facilities storing systems with PII and/or sensitive company data should have appropriate smoke and/or fire detection devices, sprinklers or approved fire suppression systems, and accessible fire extinguishers.
  • All servers containing PII and/or sensitive company data should be connected to an Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS) to prevent crashes during power outages. Servers should be configured to shut down in a controlled manner during extended outages.
  • All systems should be connected to surge protectors where feasible.
  • A user identification and password authentication mechanism shall be implemented to control user access. See Security Policy #6 – Access Control.
  • Employees who suspect any inappropriate or unauthorized use of workstations should immediately report the incident to the Security Officer.

docs.title: Scope of Policy
docs.text: This policy governs employee sanctions and disciplinary actions for The Company. All employees must comply with this policy. Demonstrated competence in the requirements of this policy is an important part of the responsibilities of every employee.

docs.title: Policy Statement
docs.text:
It is the policy of The Company to establish and implement appropriate, fair, and consistent sanctions for employees who fail to follow established policies and procedures or who commit various offenses.

Sanctions applied shall be: - Appropriate to the nature and severity of the error or offense. - Applied on an escalating scale, with less severe sanctions for less severe offenses and more severe sanctions for more severe offenses.

Offenses involving obvious illegal activity may result in notifications to appropriate law enforcement authorities.

All employee sanctions will be documented.

docs.title: Levels of Disciplinary Action
docs.text:
Based on the severity of the violation, disciplinary action may include:

  1. Verbal warning
  2. Written warning
  3. Education — training/retraining
  4. Removal of system privileges
  5. Suspension without pay
  6. Termination of employment

docs.title: Procedures
docs.text:
• Inadvertent release of PII and sensitive company data — Will be investigated; punishment will be determined by management and the extent of harm to the individual involved.

  • Unauthorized access to PII/sensitive data — Employees accessing PII and sensitive company data files that they do not have a reason to access is a violation that may result in immediate termination.

  • Blatant disregard for policies and procedures — May result in immediate termination.

  • Intentional release of PII/sensitive data — Intentional release of PII and sensitive company data to someone who should not have access to the information WILL result in immediate termination and possible prosecution.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text:
This policy defines the steps required to revoke both physical and system access to The Company's facilities and network resources.

Termination of Access: It is essential that supervisors and/or Information Technology (IT) terminate access to company facilities and systems in a timely manner to protect the information, systems, and resources. Supervisors/IT are required to terminate access immediately upon termination (or even before, when possible) of the employee, workforce member, or contractor.

docs.title: Access Revocation Requirements
docs.text:
Upon termination, the following steps must be taken:

  1. Physical access revocation — A terminated employee shall be required to surrender all keys, IDs, access cards/codes or badges, business cards, parking permits, and the like, that permit access to The Company's premises or information.

  2. Electronic access revocation — A terminated employee's physical and electronic access to PII and sensitive company data must be immediately blocked.

  3. Return of records — Terminated employees must return all records containing PII and sensitive company data, in any form, that may be in the former employee's possession (including information stored on laptops, portable devices, media, files, records, work papers, etc.).

  4. Revoke all computer, network, and data access, including:

Internal systems: - Microsoft Windows / Network Domain - Systems that store or access PII and sensitive company data - Email - Database applications - Any other systems the terminated employee has access to

External systems: - Cloud-based systems such as credit card processing, billing, CRM, etc. - Remote access must be removed - Wireless access must be removed

  1. Documentation — All termination steps that are taken should be documented and retained for legal purposes and/or federal or state regulations.

docs.title: Security Policy #1: Written Information Security Policy (WISP)
Statement of Policy
docs.text:
The objective of The Company in the development and implementation of this comprehensive Written Information Security Policy (WISP) is to create effective administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of personally identifiable information (PII) of customers, clients, and employees, as well as sensitive company information that could be harmful if unauthorized access were to occur.

The WISP sets forth a procedure for evaluating and addressing electronic and physical methods of accessing, collecting, storing, using, transmitting, and protecting PII and sensitive company information.

The use of the term employees includes all of The Company's owners, managers, employees, all independent contractors, and temporary employees.

docs.title: Purpose of Policy
docs.text:
The purpose of the WISP is to:

  1. Ensure the security and confidentiality of PII of customers, clients, employees, or vendors, as well as sensitive company data (e.g., emails, confidential company information, employee information).
  2. Protect against any reasonably anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such information.
  3. Protect against unauthorized access to or use of such information in a manner that creates a substantial risk of identity theft, fraud, or harm to The Company.

docs.title: Scope of Policy
docs.text:
In formulating and implementing the WISP, The Company has:

  • Identified reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to the security, confidentiality, and/or integrity of any electronic, paper, or other records containing PII and sensitive company data.
  • Assessed the likelihood and potential damage of these threats.
  • Evaluated the sufficiency of existing policies, procedures, and safeguards.
  • Designed and implemented a WISP that puts safeguards in place to minimize identified risks.
  • Implemented regular monitoring of the effectiveness of those safeguards.

docs.title: Administrative Safeguards
docs.text:
Security Officer — The Company has designated a Security Officer responsible for:

  • Implementation of the WISP and all provisions outlined in Security Safeguards.
  • Training of all employees who may have access to PII and sensitive company data (annual training for existing employees; training at hire for new employees).
  • Regular monitoring of the WISP's safeguards and employee compliance.
  • Evaluating Third Party Service Providers' ability to implement appropriate security measures, and requiring contractual compliance.
  • Reviewing all security measures at least annually, or whenever there is a material change in business practices.
  • Investigating, reviewing, and responding to all security incidents or suspected incidents.

Security Management — All security measures will be reviewed at least annually, including performing a security risk assessment, documenting results, and implementing recommendations.

Minimal Data Collection — The Company will only collect PII that is necessary to accomplish legitimate business transactions or comply with regulations.

Information Access — Access to records containing PII and/or sensitive company data shall be limited to persons whose job functions require legitimate access.

Employee Termination — Terminated employees must return all records containing PII and sensitive company data. Physical and electronic access must be immediately blocked. Keys, IDs, access codes/badges, and business cards must be surrendered. See Security Policy #2 – Termination Policy.

Security Training — All employees will receive annual security training. New employees should be trained as part of the onboarding process. Employees must pass an exam demonstrating their knowledge. Documentation of training should be retained.

WISP Distribution — A copy of the WISP is to be distributed to each current and new employee. Employees must acknowledge receipt in writing or electronically. See Appendix A – WISP Employee Acknowledgement Form.

Contingency Planning — All systems storing PII and/or sensitive company data should have data backed up nightly, encrypted, and stored offsite. Disaster Recovery mechanisms and documented procedures should be in place. A system criticality assessment should define how critical each system is. Backups and recovery procedures should be tested periodically.

Security Incident Procedures — Employees are required to report suspicious or unauthorized use of PII and/or sensitive company data. Post-incident reviews must be conducted. See Security Policy #3 – Security Incident Response.

Emergency Operations — Procedures should define how The Company will respond to emergencies, including employee contact information, critical vendor contacts, account information, and emergency operating procedures.

Data Sensitivity Classification — All data should be categorized by its sensitivity level. PII and sensitive company data should be highly protected; publicly accessible information requires minimal protection.

Third Party Service Providers — Any service provider permitted access to PII and/or sensitive company data must be required to protect it. Service agreements must contractually hold them responsible.

Sanctions — Disciplinary actions will be taken for violations. See Security Policy #4 – Sanction Policy.

BYOD Policy — If employees are allowed to use personally owned devices, proper safeguards must be implemented. See Security Policy #9 – BYOD Policy.

docs.title: Physical Safeguards
docs.text:
Facility Access Controls — Physical security must be implemented to prevent unauthorized access. All systems accessing or storing PII must be physically locked. Employees must maintain a "clean desk" and properly secure PII when away from their desk. Visitors will be restricted from areas containing PII. See Security Policy #10 – Facility Security Plan.

Network Security — Safeguards include: isolating systems that access PII, encryption on all portable devices, anti-malware on all systems, network firewalls, periodic vulnerability scans, capturing and retaining network log files, and ensuring servers are stored in an environmentally safe location. See Security Policy #5 – Network Security.

docs.title: Technical Safeguards
docs.text:
Access Control — Access to PII and sensitive company data shall be restricted to approved active users and accounts only. Unique user accounts and passwords must be assigned. Automatic logoff procedures must be implemented. See Security Policy #6 – Access Control.

Computer Use — All employees will be given a Computer Use Policy defining acceptable and unacceptable use of computing resources. See Security Policy #7 – Computer Use.

Data Disposal — Written and electronic records containing PII shall be securely destroyed or deleted at the earliest opportunity consistent with business needs or legal retention requirements. See Security Policy #8 – Equipment Disposal.

System Activity Review — All systems storing or accessing PII should log and store system activity. Periodic reviews should identify unauthorized access. See Security Policy #3 – Security Incident Response.

Encryption — To the extent technically feasible, all portable devices containing PII should be encrypted. Encryption should also be used when sending PII across public networks, email, and wireless networks.

docs.title: Appendix A — WISP Employee Acknowledgement Form
docs.text:
I have read, understand, and agree to comply with the Written Information Security Policy (WISP), rules, and conditions governing the security of PII and sensitive company data. I am aware that violations of the WISP may subject me to disciplinary action and may include termination of my employment.

By signing this Agreement, I agree to comply with its terms and conditions. Failure to read this Agreement is not an excuse for violating it.

docs.title: SOP: Backup Nonstandard Directories to OneDrive for Business
docs.text: This SOP provides PowerShell commands and guidance for backing up non-standard directories (outside of Desktop, Documents, and Pictures) to OneDrive for Business using Copy-Item.

docs.title: Back up a directory in the user profile:
docs.text: Copy-Item -Path C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\directory* -Recurse -Destination C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\"OneDrive - {{COMPANY}}"\backupdirectory\

docs.title: Back up a directory from Program Files:
docs.text: Copy-Item -Path C:\"Program Files (x86)"\directory* -Recurse -Destination C:\Users\$env:USERNAME\"OneDrive - {{COMPANY}}"\backupdirectory\

docs.title: Important Notes
docs.text:
• Spaces in directory names — Any directory with spaces in its name must be enclosed in quotation marks.
  • Root-level backups — You can back up folders to the root of OneDrive (not inside Desktop/Documents/Pictures) to help protect users from accidentally deleting backed-up data.
  • -Recurse flag — This flag is necessary to ensure all subfolders and files are copied. Do not omit it.
  • {{COMPANY}} placeholder — This is not a valid flag. You must manually determine the exact name of the client's OneDrive folder and put it in quotation marks. For example: "OneDrive - Imperitiv Solutions"
  • $env:USERNAME — This flag pulls the currently logged-on user. This script will not work if you need to back up files stored in one user's directory to a different user's OneDrive.

docs.title: SOP: Build a PC Using Intune (Windows Autopilot)
docs.text: This SOP describes the steps required to provision a new or existing PC using Microsoft Intune and Windows Autopilot.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to Intune/Azure portal with Global Admin or Intune Service Admin credentials.
  • The device must have Internet connectivity.
  • The device's hardware ID must be registered in Intune (via CSV upload or OEM injection).

docs.title: Part 1: Register the Hardware ID
docs.text:
1. Order the laptop/desktop through a VAR or direct from the OEM. Ensure the VAR/OEM either:
  2. Provides the hardware ID in a CSV file for upload into Intune, or

  3. Injects the hardware ID into Intune directly.

  4. If the hardware ID is not already in Intune and is not provided by the VAR/OEM, run the PowerShell scripts on the Windows machine using one of the two methods below.

docs.title: Option A: Device is in OOBE (Out-of-Box Experience)
docs.text:
1. Turn on the PC and wait for the OOBE to start. Do not proceed through any OOBE steps.
  2. Ensure a network cable is plugged in and the PC has Internet connectivity.
  3. If only Wi-Fi is available, proceed through OOBE only until the Wi-Fi setup step. Once connected, do not proceed further.
  4. Press and hold Shift + F10 to open a CMD prompt.
  5. At the CMD prompt, type PowerShell and press Enter.
  6. Execute: Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted
  7. Execute: Install-Script -Name Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo
  8. Answer Yes or Y to all prompts that follow.
  9. Execute: Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo.ps1 -TenantName "%tenantname%.onmicrosoft.com" (replace %tenantname% with your tenant name).
  10. Authenticate when prompted — you must be a Global Admin or Intune Service Admin.
  11. When the script completes successfully, power off the PC.

docs.title: Option B: Device is Already in Use but Not in Intune
docs.text:
1. Log in to the Windows machine using an admin account.
  2. Ensure the PC has Internet connectivity.
  3. Launch a PowerShell window as an administrator.
  4. Execute: Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted
  5. Execute: Install-Script -Name Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo
  6. Answer Yes or Y to all prompts.
  7. Execute: Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo.ps1 -TenantName "%tenantname%.onmicrosoft.com" (replace %tenantname% with your tenant name).
  8. Authenticate when prompted — you must be a Global Admin or Intune Service Admin.
  9. When the script completes successfully, power off the PC.

docs.title: Part 2: Add the Device to Intune Groups
docs.text:
1. Log in to the Intune portal at https://portal.azure.com.
  2. Add the device to the appropriate device security group (e.g., SG-IAP-City).

docs.title: Part 3: Assign the Device to a User (Optional)
docs.text:
If you know who the PC will be assigned to, assign the intended user:

  1. Go to the Intune "Windows Autopilot Devices" blade.
  2. Click the checkbox next to the machine you need to assign.
  3. Click "Assign user" from the menu.
  4. Type the user's name into the "Select" box, click their name to select them, then click the "Select" button.
  5. IMPORTANT: Ensure the "Profile status" on this screen shows "Assigned". The machine cannot be used until this status is reached. If you encounter issues, contact Imperitiv Support.

Note: If you do not know who the PC will be assigned to, you can stop here and place the PC in storage. Be sure to label the machine indicating its current state.

docs.title: Part 4: Issue the PC to the User
docs.text:
1. Hand the computer to the user.
  2. Have them plug in power, attach a network cable (or use Wi-Fi), and turn on the PC.
  3. When the OOBE screen appears with an Ethernet connection, the user should be able to flow through the steps and be prompted by name to log in to the Azure AD instance.
  4. If using Wi-Fi, the user will need to configure their Wi-Fi connection during OOBE before being prompted to log in with their company account.

docs.title: SOP: Mobile Device Enrollment in MDM (2022)
Overview
docs.text:
If you do not enroll your mobile device with Microsoft Endpoint Manager (MEM), formerly named Intune, you will not be able to access company email or data on your mobile device.

Important Note: Enrolling your phone will not give the company the ability to wipe your personal data, control your phone, or retrieve your phone number. It creates a "sandbox" on your phone where apps and accounts held in that sandbox can securely interact with company email and cloud data.

The MEM Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution will: - Prevent you from copying company data outside of the sandbox of managed apps on your device. - Require you to install and use approved apps to read email (Outlook mobile app) and interact with data (OneDrive, SharePoint).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before beginning enrollment:

  • If you already have your company email account on your phone, remove it first. Directions for removing account
  • Install the Company Portal App from your appropriate App Store. Read through the enrollment directions before setting it up or logging in.
  • Read the full directions and watch the relevant video before executing the steps.

docs.title: iPhone Enrollment
docs.text:
• Video: Enroll your iOS device in Microsoft Intune
  • Directions: Set up iOS device access to your company resources — Microsoft Learn

docs.title: Android Enrollment
docs.text:
• Video: Set up Android Work Profile on your device using Microsoft Intune — YouTube
  • Directions: Enroll device and create Android work profile — Microsoft Docs

docs.title: SOP: Manage Firefox Settings in Microsoft Endpoint Manager (MEM)
docs.text: This SOP describes how to create Mozilla Firefox extension policies using Microsoft Endpoint Manager (Intune) and custom OMA-URI settings.

docs.title: Steps to Create a Firefox Extension Policy
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Microsoft Endpoint Manager > Devices > Configuration Profiles > Create Profile.

  2. Set:

  3. Platform: Windows 10 and later

  4. Profile type: Templates > Custom

  5. Name the profile appropriately — for example: [Company Name] – Extension Policy – Firefox

  6. Add an OMA-URI setting by following the instructions at: Managing Firefox with Microsoft Endpoint Manager (Intune) | Firefox for Enterprise Help

  7. The OMA-URI setting should look like the following: ./Device/Vendor/MSFT/Policy/ConfigOperations/ADMXInstall/Firefox/Policy/FirefoxAdmx

  8. For Data Type, select String and paste the entire contents of the Firefox ADMX file into the value field.

  9. Download the ADMX file from: mozilla/policy-templates Releases on GitHub

  10. Open the downloaded ADMX file in Notepad and copy all of its text.

  11. Save the profile.

docs.title: Adding Specific Firefox Policy Settings
docs.text:
To add specific Firefox settings after the ADMX has been ingested:

  1. Go back into the extension policy profile.
  2. Under "Configuration Settings", click Edit.
  3. Add additional settings from the policy template reference: policy-templates README on GitHub

docs.title: SOP: Migrate a PC from On-Premises AD to Azure AD
docs.text: This SOP outlines the step-by-step process for migrating a Windows PC from an on-premises Active Directory (AD) domain to Azure Active Directory (Azure AD).

docs.title: Stage I — Register Device with Autopilot
docs.text:
1. Open PowerShell as an administrator and run the following commands:

powershell Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted Install-Script Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo Upload-WindowsAutopilotDeviceInfo -TenantName XXXXX.OnMicrosoft.com

  1. Authenticate with the Imperitiv admin account for the client tenant.
  2. Ensure successful execution of the command.
  3. Reboot the computer.
  4. On reboot, enter the BIOS and verify:
  5. Secure Boot is enabled.
  6. The computer has a TPM and it is enabled.

docs.title: Stage II — Prepare the User's Data
docs.text:
Have the user who is being migrated log in, then complete the following:

  1. Ensure Desktop, Documents, and Pictures folders are being synced with OneDrive (for the target tenant) and that the Backup option is enabled and successful for all three folders.
  2. Have the user review their Downloads folder for anything they need to retain.
  3. Verify browser bookmarks (Chrome/Firefox/Edge, etc.) are exported and saved to OneDrive and synchronized.
  4. PST files:
  5. Check Outlook for any attached PST files.
  6. Verify the size and that the data will fit in Office 365.
  7. Migrate any PST data to the user's O365 mailbox.
  8. If the PST data is too large to migrate within the allotted time:
    1. Record the path to the PST.
    2. Detach the PST from the Outlook profile.
    3. Close Outlook.
    4. Move the PST to a location such as C:\Outlook Files\.
    5. After migration, move the PST back to the user's profile (C:\Users\%USERNAME%) and reattach it to Outlook.
    6. Schedule a time to help the user migrate the PST data to the Exchange Online mailbox.
  9. Open Device Manager and verify there are no issues with drivers.

docs.title: Stage III — Disjoin from On-Premises AD and Join Azure AD
docs.text:
1. Create a local computer user in Computer Management (e.g., imp-admin) and make it a member of the local Administrators group.
  2. Log out the current user and log in as imp-admin.
  3. Disjoin the computer from the on-premises AD domain and reboot the computer.
  4. Log in as imp-admin after the reboot.
  5. Join the PC to Azure Active Directory using the user's account:
  6. Go to Settings > Accounts > Access Work or School.
  7. Click Connect.
  8. Click Join this device to Azure Active Directory.
  9. Have the user enter their email address (user ID) for the new tenant.
  10. Have them enter their password.
  11. Click Next.
  12. Click Join.
  13. Click Done and then reboot the computer.

docs.title: Stage IV — First Login as Azure AD Account
docs.text:
1. Log in to the machine using the user's Azure AD account: %USERNAME%@DomainName.com.
  2. The "Setting up your device" window will appear. This may take several minutes.
  3. If the first two sections do not show "Completed", the machine did not receive the Disable ESP Configuration policy. Force reboots and policy updates until it does.
  4. If Windows Hello is enabled, the user will be prompted to set a PIN and may be able to configure biometrics (fingerprint or facial recognition if supported).

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
After the user logs in:

  1. Let the machine sit for 5–6 minutes and wait for OneDrive to set up and begin syncing automatically.
  2. Verify OneDrive sets up and syncs.
  3. Select "Always keep on this device" for OneDrive files.
  4. Set up and verify email access.
  5. Import bookmarks.
  6. Move any Downloads data retained from Stage II.

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
1. Ensure the user is not a local administrator.
  2. Delete C:\Temp (if created) or any other temp folders.
  3. Rename the Azure AD computer object and force a reboot (naming convention: CompanyCode-SerialNumber).
  4. Have the user log back in and verify SharePoint Document Libraries are mapped.
  5. Open Computer Management as an Admin and disable the user's local account so they cannot accidentally switch back to it. Do not delete it.

docs.title: Error 8018000a
docs.text:
Message: "Something went wrong. The device is already enrolled. You can contact your system administrator with the error code 8018000a."

Resolution:

  1. Use Process Monitor to identify traces from an "ongoing Azure AD join."
  2. Delete all GUIDs under: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Enrollments
  3. Do NOT delete the following keys: Context, Ownership, Status, and ValidNodePaths.

Reference: https://jocha.se/blog/tech/azure-ad-mdm-intune-error-8018000a
